- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui people were first inhabited by Polynesian settlers between 800 and 1200 CE** — The Easter Island natives, also known as the Rapa Nui people, were first inhabited by Polynesian settlers sometime between 800 and 1200 CE.
- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui people developed a unique culture known for the Moai statues** — The descendants of the Polynesian settlers developed a unique culture and society, known for their expert craftsmanship in creating the famous Moai statues scattered across the island.
- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui religion centered on ancestor worship and treated the Moai as ancestors** — Their religion centered around ancestor worship, and they believed their ancestors had supernatural powers and that the Moai statues were representations of these ancestors.
- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui carved Moai from local volcanic rock and transported them with ropes and rollers** — The Moai statues were carved from local volcanic rock and transported across the island using a system of ropes and rollers.
- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui used logs, ropes, groups of people, levers, and wedges to move Moai statues** — It is believed that the Rapa Nui people used logs as rollers, several groups of individuals to pull the statues using ropes, and a system of levers and wedges to lift and move the statues into place.
- [2023-04-19] **European explorers discovered the Rapa Nui people in the 18th century** — During the 18th century, the Rapa Nui people were discovered by European explorers.
- [2023-04-19] **Chile annexed Easter Island in the late 19th century and Rapa Nui people lost lands and were forced into labor** — The island was annexed by Chile in the late 19th century, and the Rapa Nui people were subjected to forced labor and a loss of their traditional lands.
- [2023-04-19] **Many Moai statues were destroyed or damaged during the colonial period** — Many of the Moai statues were destroyed or damaged during this period after European contact and Chilean annexation.
- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui society declined after European contact and Chilean annexation** — Their society began to decline during the period of European discovery and later Chilean annexation.
- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui people have worked in recent decades to revive their culture and traditions** — In recent decades, the Rapa Nui people have been working to revive their culture and traditions.
- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui people embraced eco-tourism and preservation of Moai, language, and crafts** — They have embraced eco-tourism and worked to preserve and restore the remaining Moai statues, as well as their language and traditional crafts.
- [2023-04-19] **Rapa Nui people are now an important part of Chile's cultural heritage** — Today, the Rapa Nui people are an important part of Chile's cultural heritage and continue to celebrate their unique history and traditions.
